title: Grammar · German A1
scope: By the end of A1 you can use the present tense (Präsens) of regular verbs and of sein/haben, articles in the nominative and accusative, negation with nicht and kein, basic modal verbs, possessives, simple imperatives and separable verbs. Everything happens in the present: the past tense and the dative case are not covered yet.

blocks.text: Personal pronouns replace the subject. German has three words for 'you': du (informal), ihr (informal plural) and Sie (formal, always capitalised).
Remove the infinitive ending -en (lernen → lern-) and add the ending for each person.
If the stem ends in -t or -d, an extra -e- is added: du arbeitest, er findet.
Ich lerne Deutsch. Du wohnst in Berlin. Wir spielen Fußball.
The two most important verbs are irregular and worth memorising.
Ich bin müde. Sie hat einen Hund.
Every noun has a gender (masculine, feminine or neuter). The definite article means 'the' and the indefinite 'a/an'. In the plural the definite article is always die.
der Tisch, die Lampe, das Buch · ein Tisch, eine Lampe, ein Buch
The nominative is the subject; the accusative is the direct object. Only the masculine article changes in the accusative.

blocks.label: Only the masculine changes
blocks.text: Der Mann liest einen Roman. Ich sehe den Tisch.
kein negates nouns that have an indefinite article or no article; nicht negates verbs, adjectives and everything else.

rows: negates the verb or a complement
Ich komme nicht. Das ist nicht teuer.

blocks.text: Questions with a question word put it first and the verb in second position. In yes/no questions the verb comes first.

items: W-Fragen: wer, was, wo, wann, warum, wie, woher, wohin
Yes/no question: the verb opens the sentence

blocks.text: Wo wohnst du? — Wann beginnt der Kurs? — Kommst du mit?
Modal verbs are conjugated in second position and the main verb goes to the end of the sentence as an infinitive.

blocks.label: Verb at the end
blocks.text: Ich kann gut schwimmen. Wir müssen jetzt gehen.
They show possession and are declined like ein/kein. The ending agrees with the thing possessed, not with the owner.
Das ist mein Bruder. Wo ist deine Tasche?
Many verbs have a separable prefix (an-, auf-, ein-, mit-, aus-…). In the present tense the prefix splits off and goes to the end of the sentence.
aufstehen → Ich stehe um 7 Uhr auf. · einkaufen → Wir kaufen am Samstag ein.

sections.title: Word order: the verb in second position

blocks.text: In a statement the conjugated verb is always in second position, even if the sentence starts with another element (time, place…).
Heute lerne ich Deutsch. (not: Heute ich lerne …)
